stories.headline: Colorado overhauls state IT office, lays off 173 employees amid poor performance reviews
stories.summary:
The Governor's Office of Information Technology announced a major operational realignment Wednesday, laying off 173 employees — roughly 15% of its 1,150-person workforce. The restructuring is projected to save $4 million in its first full year and $8 million annually thereafter, and was driven not by budget pressure or artificial intelligence but by persistently poor customer satisfaction scores and repeated criticism from the state auditor and legislative oversight bodies.

OIT Executive Director David Edinger acknowledged that satisfaction scores had barely budged during his tenure, moving from 31% to 36% against a target of 67%. Edinger's last day is June 11, when he will hand off leadership to deputy director Sarah Tuneberg.

The agency manages cybersecurity for state digital networks and supports government services used by millions of Coloradans, including unemployment benefits, SNAP, Medicaid and tax refund tracking. Lawmakers, including Rep. Brianna Titone of Arvada, have also raised questions about a $36 million cash fund that OIT accumulated, which she said suggests the department was overcharging state agencies for services. OIT said the funds are being returned to the state general fund.

stories.headline: Sen. Bennet proposes law to block new ICE detention centers in Colorado as Hudson facility fate remains uncertain
stories.summary:
U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet stood outside the shuttered Hudson Correctional Facility northeast of Denver on Tuesday to announce legislation aimed at giving local governments authority to block new immigration detention centers in Colorado. Bennet, who is running for the Democratic gubernatorial nomination, said he wants to expand a 52-year-old state law that grants local governments planning authority over projects of statewide impact — such as airports and mines — to also cover detention facilities.

The Hudson facility has been eyed by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement since last year as part of the federal government's push to dramatically expand detention capacity nationwide. ICE issued a nearly $40 million letter contract to the private prison company Geo Group for the site, which the agency has called the 'Big Horn Correctional Facility,' but its status remains unresolved months later.

Bennet acknowledged it would be 'very hard' to stop the Hudson facility specifically, since it sits on privately owned land and has previously operated as a prison. Colorado currently has one immigration detention center, an Aurora facility also operated by Geo Group. ICE has said it wants to add hundreds more detention beds in Colorado, and planning documents have also identified a shuttered prison in Walsenburg as a potential expansion site.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis appears on Democratic Party Zoom with tape over his mouth, one week after censure
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis appeared during a Colorado Democratic Party Zoom meeting Wednesday with tape over his mouth, a gesture that came just one week after party leaders censured him for granting clemency to convicted election clerk Tina Peters. The visual display drew immediate attention given the fraught relationship between Polis and party leadership following the censure vote.

Polis's decision to pardon Peters, who was convicted of tampering with voting equipment in Mesa County, sparked widespread criticism from within his own party. The censure was a formal rebuke, and Wednesday's Zoom appearance suggested the governor was responding to the conflict in a pointed, if theatrical, manner.

Details about what was said during the meeting and any reaction from party officials were not immediately available.

stories.headline: State Sen. Julie Gonzales challenges 'do-nothing' Democrats in U.S. Senate primary against Hickenlooper
stories.summary:
State Sen. Julie Gonzales is mounting a progressive challenge against former Gov. John Hickenlooper in the Democratic primary for Colorado's U.S. Senate seat, framing her campaign around dissatisfaction with establishment Democrats she describes as ineffective. Gonzales outlined her argument in a recent interview with CPR News, saying she is running to disrupt what she calls 'do-nothing' Democrats in the Senate.

The June 30 primary will determine which Democrat faces the Republican nominee in the general election for the Senate seat. Gonzales, a Denver-area legislator, is positioning herself as a contrast to Hickenlooper's more centrist record and national profile.

The race is one of several competitive Democratic primaries in Colorado this cycle, including the gubernatorial contest between Bennet and Attorney General Phil Weiser.

stories.headline: Trial opens for Jefferson County man accused of shooting teen in the face after boys knocked on barn door
stories.summary:
A jury trial began Tuesday in Jefferson County for Brent Metz, 40, a former elected official in the town of Mountain View, who is accused of shooting a 17-year-old in the face in September 2024. According to prosecutors, two teenagers drove to a property in Conifer to scout a location for homecoming photos and knocked on a barn door before leaving without incident — at which point Metz arrived, blocked their parked car with his truck, and fired a single shot through their windshield.

The bullet struck Jack Howard in the nose and lodged behind his eye. Metz faces four felony counts: second-degree assault, illegal discharge of a firearm and two counts of felony menacing. His defense attorney argued the gun discharged unintentionally, while prosecutors said Metz deliberately confronted the teenagers while armed.

Prosecutors told jurors that Howard was sitting in the car writing a note to the homeowners when Metz fired. The case is being prosecuted by the Jefferson County District Attorney's Office.

stories.headline: Cyclist killed after being struck by vehicle while crossing U.S. 85 against red light in Brighton
stories.summary:
A cyclist was struck and killed late Tuesday night while crossing U.S. 85 at East 120th Avenue in Brighton, according to the Brighton Police Department. The crash occurred at approximately 11:30 p.m. when the cyclist crossed the highway against a red light and was hit by a northbound vehicle.

The cyclist was transported to Platte Valley Hospital, where they were pronounced dead. The driver remained at the scene and showed no signs of impairment, police said. The victim's identity will be released by the Adams County Coroner's Office.

Anyone with information is asked to contact the Brighton Police Department tip line at 303-655-8740.

stories.headline: Colorado defense attorneys revolt against new leader of Office of Alternate Defense Counsel over cost-cutting culture
stories.summary:
More than 200 Colorado criminal defense attorneys and staff gathered at a two-hour public meeting Friday to voice alarm about the new executive director of the Office of the Alternate Defense Counsel, Joanna Landau, who was hired in September. Attorneys described a culture of fear within the agency and said Landau's emphasis on cost-cutting could undermine the constitutional rights of indigent defendants the office is charged with representing.

The ADC contracts with roughly 1,200 private attorneys and support staff statewide to represent defendants who cannot be served by the Office of the State Public Defender, typically due to conflicts of interest. Veteran Denver defense attorney James Castle told the oversight commission that attorneys had 'never had a more heightened level of concern about ADC leadership' in four decades of practice.

Critics said Landau imposed a strict chain-of-command structure and limited communication between staff and contracted attorneys — a sharp departure from the collaborative approach of her predecessor, Lindy Frolich, who led the agency for 20 years. Landau did not respond to requests for comment. The nine-member volunteer commission that oversees the ADC hosted the meeting.

stories.headline: Broomfield quantum computing company Quantinuum seeks $1 billion in IPO at over $12 billion valuation
stories.summary:
Quantinuum, the Broomfield-based quantum computing company, priced shares this week as it seeks to raise $1 billion in an initial public offering at a valuation exceeding $12 billion. The IPO comes as the federal government is reportedly considering buying a stake in the business.

Quantinuum is one of the most prominent technology companies headquartered in the Denver metro area and has positioned itself as a leader in the emerging quantum computing industry. The company was formed from the merger of Honeywell Quantum Solutions and Cambridge Quantum.

The IPO, if completed at the targeted valuation, would represent one of the largest public market debuts by a Colorado-based company in recent years.

stories.headline: Glendale pushes back on Colorado Boulevard bus rapid transit plan, citing traffic and business impacts
stories.summary:
The city of Glendale has passed a resolution urging the 'no-build' option for the proposed Colorado Boulevard bus rapid transit project, becoming a notable point of opposition within a corridor that stretches 7 miles through east Denver. Glendale, an enclave of 4,500 residents completely surrounded by Denver, argues that converting general-purpose vehicle lanes to bus-only lanes would cause severe traffic disruption and eliminate left turns critical to local businesses.

The Colorado Department of Transportation, which oversees the state highway, is in early planning stages for the $280 million project running from East 40th Avenue to East Yale Avenue. CDOT estimates the most robust BRT option could cut projected 2045 bus travel times by 30-35% and nearly double daily ridership from around 2,800 trips. About 1 mile of the 7-mile corridor runs through Glendale, which currently sees close to 50,000 vehicle trips per day.

The Colorado Boulevard project is one of several BRT initiatives advancing in metro Denver. Construction on the $280 million East Colfax BRT project has crossed into Aurora, a Federal Boulevard corridor is being planned for similar treatment, and a Diagonal Highway BRT between Boulder and Longmont is partially complete.

stories.headline: Fan Expo Denver brings largest-ever X-Files reunion, Lord of the Rings cast to Colorado Convention Center
stories.summary:
Fan Expo Denver opens Thursday at the Colorado Convention Center, drawing an expected 100,000 attendees over four days with one of the most star-studded lineups in the event's history. Highlights include a 25th anniversary reunion of the 'Lord of the Rings' cast featuring Orlando Bloom, Elijah Wood, Sean Astin and others, as well as what organizers are billing as the largest-ever 'X-Files' reunion, with Gillian Anderson, David Duchovny and five additional cast members marking the show's 33rd anniversary.

Other appearances include casts from 'The Boys,' 'Scream,' 'The Goonies,' 'Doctor Who,' 'The Karate Kid' and 'Rocky IV,' along with dozens of additional actors from film and television franchises. Actor and DJ Elijah Wood's 'Wooden Wisdom' vinyl project will also perform at Cervantes' Masterpiece Ballroom in a Middle-earth-themed event Sunday night.

Of local note, Duchovny is appearing in a new film written by Denver comedian and actor Adam Cayton-Holland, titled 'See You When I See You,' about a family coping after a suicide. The film is set for release this fall.

stories.headline: Maroon Bells management could shift to Pitkin County as Forest Service faces staffing and funding shortfall
stories.summary:
The U.S. Forest Service is considering issuing a special-use permit that would transfer day-to-day management of the Maroon Bells Scenic Area near Aspen to Pitkin County, citing an ongoing gap between the site's $600,000 annual operating costs and roughly $220,000 in fee revenue. Federal staffing cuts last year resulted in bathrooms, water and electricity not being fully available to the approximately 191,000 visitors who came to the scenic area between May and October.

Pitkin County's Open Space and Trails director Gary Tennenbaum said county commissioners have expressed interest in taking on the responsibility, viewing it as part of a broader effort to backstop federal land management reductions. Under the proposed arrangement, fees — currently $10 for parking reservations and $16 for adult shuttle rides — would likely increase, since a county permittee has more flexibility to adjust fees than the Forest Service.

The Maroon Bells arrangement reflects a wider trend in the White River National Forest, where local governments and concessionaires are increasingly stepping in to fill gaps left by reduced federal capacity. An operating plan would need approval from both the Forest Service and Pitkin County before taking effect.

stories.headline: Avalanche face major offseason questions after stunning sweep by Vegas Golden Knights
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche's season ended Tuesday in a four-game sweep by the Vegas Golden Knights in the Western Conference Final, a result that players described in the locker room as humiliating and wasteful after a dominant regular season in which the team won the most games and scored the most goals in the NHL. Colorado scored just seven goals across the four games and saw its two marquee streaks — 45-0 when leading after two periods and 52-0 when building a multi-goal lead at any point — broken by a Vegas team that had fired its head coach 51 days before the series began.

The defeat raises sharp questions about the future of the franchise's core, including head coach Jared Bednar and general manager Chris MacFarland, with the team projected to have the smallest salary cap cushion in the NHL heading into next season. Key players including Nathan MacKinnon and Cale Makar were dealing with injuries during the series, and the roster's aging profile — with multiple core players in their mid-to-late 30s — complicates the team's window for another championship run.

Forward Logan O'Connor said the result 'just feels like a waste,' noting the team had entered the season with a 'Cup or bust' mentality. The Avalanche won the Stanley Cup in 2022 but have been eliminated in the playoffs each year since, including a gut-wrenching Game 7 loss to Dallas a year ago.

weather.notable: Nearly 8 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: Where lipstick is concerned, the important thing is not color, but to accept God's final word on where your lips end.

topStory.headline: U.S. and Iran Exchange Fresh Strikes Near Strait of Hormuz as Trump Resists Pressure to Settle
topStory.summary:
American forces carried out new strikes against Iranian targets near the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday, shooting down four Iranian one-way attack drones and destroying a ground control station in Bandar Abbas that was preparing to launch a fifth drone. Hours later, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps announced it had targeted a U.S. air base it identified as the source of the American attacks, though the Pentagon had not publicly confirmed that strike. Kuwait's military separately announced its air defenses were intercepting hostile missiles and drones, raising the possibility that Iran targeted the U.S. air installation hosted on Kuwaiti soil.

President Donald Trump, speaking at a Cabinet meeting Wednesday, said Iran was "negotiating on fumes" and dismissed suggestions that he would accelerate diplomatic efforts due to economic pressure or the approach of November midterm elections. Trump also warned Oman not to partner with Iran in any arrangement to jointly control the Strait of Hormuz, saying the country would face military consequences if it did not comply. He separately suggested the U.S. might withhold progress on an Iran deal unless additional Gulf nations joined the Abraham Accords and normalized relations with Israel.

The latest exchange marks the second time in three days that U.S. forces have carried out what the Pentagon has described as defensive strikes against Iran. The Strait of Hormuz, which Iran has effectively closed since a U.S.-Israeli military campaign began in late February, remains shut to commercial shipping, contributing to rising global prices for oil, natural gas, fertilizer and other commodities. U.S. officials said Wednesday's strikes were limited in scope and did not constitute a resumption of large-scale combat operations.

Negotiations to end the nearly three-month-old conflict remain unsettled. Any emerging framework is expected to address the reopening of the Strait and some reduction of Iran's nuclear capabilities, though analysts and some Republican critics have warned that key issues are being deferred rather than resolved. Trump faces pressure from multiple directions: international economic fallout from the closed strait, domestic political opposition, and the challenge of presenting any eventual agreement as a credible victory.
